Project Brief & Help

AI-powered incident analysis from logs to remediation

The Multi-Agent DevOps Incident Analysis Suite helps DevOps and SRE teams detect incidents, identify likely root causes, retrieve relevant enterprise procedures, generate actionable runbooks, and coordinate Jira and Slack responses.

6specialized AI agents
2knowledge sources
1incremental vector index
End-to-endtraceable incident flow

1. Project Overview

This application is designed for DevOps Engineers, SREs, Incident Managers, Platform Engineers, Team Leads, and Operations teams. Users upload application or infrastructure logs, and the system orchestrates a structured incident-analysis workflow through LangGraph.

Log Analysis Root Cause Analysis RAG Recommendations Runbook Generation

Primary outcomes

  • Detect and display all incidents found in uploaded logs.
  • Classify incident severity and extract service, timestamp, category, and evidence.
  • Generate a probable root cause with confidence and alternative causes.
  • Retrieve relevant Confluence pages and local SOP content from LanceDB.
  • Produce remediation steps, executable commands, validation, and rollback guidance.
  • Create Jira tickets and send Slack notifications according to severity policy.
Operational safety: Generated commands and remediation steps must be reviewed by an authorized engineer before being executed in production.
Back to top ↑

2. Architecture Overview

The architecture combines a Next.js frontend, FastAPI backend, LangGraph multi-agent workflow, LanceDB vector storage, Confluence and local SOP ingestion, Clerk authentication, and Jira/Slack integrations.

Architectural flow: authentication and log upload feed the multi-agent backend, while the RAG pipeline supplies source-attributed evidence to remediation.
Back to top ↑

3. End-to-End Workflow

1. Upload Log.log or .txt
2. Detect IncidentLog Reader
3. Analyze CauseRCA Agent
4. Find FixesRemediation + RAG
5. Build RunbookCookbook Agent
6. CoordinateJira + Slack

How to use the application

  1. Sign in using the configured Clerk authentication flow.
  2. Open Upload Logs and upload a supported log file.
  3. Wait for the Log Reader Agent to detect all incidents.
  4. Select an incident from the incident list.
  5. Review the RCA, recommendations, supporting sources, and cookbook.
  6. For non-critical incidents, create a Jira ticket manually when required.
  7. For critical incidents, verify the automatically generated Jira and Slack outcome.
Back to top ↑

4. Agent Responsibilities

1Log Reader Agent

Parses uploaded logs and detects all incidents without creating tickets or notifications.

  • Extracts timestamps, services, categories, and raw evidence.
  • Assigns severity and confidence.
  • Returns a structured incident list.

2RCA Agent

Determines the most likely root cause using the incident evidence.

  • Primary cause
  • Alternative causes
  • Evidence and confidence

3Remediation Agent

Queries LanceDB for relevant SOPs and Confluence knowledge, then generates ranked recommendations.

  • Recommended fix
  • Rationale and confidence
  • Supporting source references
  • Structured Jira payload preparation

4Cookbook Agent

Converts the selected remediation into an executable operational runbook.

  • Step-by-step actions
  • Commands
  • Validation checks
  • Rollback procedure

5Notification Agent

Sends a structured Slack summary when incident policy requires notification.

6Jira Agent

Creates a Jira issue using the prepared payload for incidents that meet the configured policy.

Back to top ↑

5. Severity Handling

SeveritySystem behaviorUser action
LowIncident is displayed and analyzed.Review and monitor.
MediumRCA, remediation, and cookbook are generated.Create Jira manually if needed.
HighRCA, remediation, and cookbook are generated.Follow configured Jira policy.
CriticalFull workflow, automatic Jira creation, and Slack notification.Verify and coordinate response.
The Log Reader Agent detects and classifies incidents only. It does not create Jira tickets or send Slack messages.
Back to top ↑

6. RAG Pipeline

Retrieval-Augmented Generation grounds remediation recommendations in approved organizational documentation. 

Confluence + Local SOP Directory
            ↓
Discover → Load → Normalize → Chunk → Embed → Store
            ↓
          LanceDB
            ↓
Retriever → Remediation Agent → Source-attributed Recommendations

Supported knowledge sources

  • Confluence Cloud: configured spaces and pages accessed through the REST API.
  • Local SOP directory: approved Markdown, text, PDF, and DOCX documents.

Incremental synchronization

  • New and changed documents are parsed, chunked, embedded, and indexed.
  • Unchanged documents are skipped.
  • Changed document chunks replace only the previous chunks for that document.
  • Removed documents are marked unavailable according to the configured policy.
Back to top ↑

7. RAG Configuration

Knowledge Index Synchronization

The RAG Configuration page should provide a manual Sync Knowledge Base button.

FieldMeaning
Last successful synchronizationThe most recent sync that completed successfully.
Last synchronization attemptThe latest manual or scheduled attempt, including failures.
Index createdThe date and time the LanceDB index was initially created.
Index last updatedThe latest time the indexed content actually changed.
Next scheduled synchronizationThe next weekly run calculated by the backend scheduler.
Indexed documentsTotal active Confluence pages and local SOP files.
Indexed chunksTotal active chunks available for retrieval.

Manual synchronization

  • Sync Knowledge Base: synchronizes both Confluence and Local SOP sources.
  • Sync Confluence: optionally synchronizes only configured Confluence pages.
  • Sync Local SOPs: optionally synchronizes only the local SOP directory.

Weekly synchronization

When enabled, the backend scheduler runs the same incremental synchronization coordinator on a weekly schedule. The frontend must display the next scheduled execution date from the backend.

A manual synchronization does not reset or postpone the weekly schedule. Manual and scheduled jobs must not run concurrently.

Common parameters

ParameterDescription
Embedding modelModel used to convert chunks and queries into vectors.
Chunk targetPreferred chunk size in tokens.
Chunk maximumMaximum allowed chunk size.
Chunk overlapContext retained between adjacent chunks.
Top KMaximum number of retrieved evidence chunks.
Schedule timezoneTimezone used to calculate the next weekly synchronization.
Back to top ↑

8. Incident Details

TabPurpose
OverviewIncident summary, service, timestamp, severity, and current workflow status.
RCAPrimary root cause, confidence, evidence, and alternative causes.
Recommended StepsRanked remediation options with rationale and sources.
CookbookExecutable actions, commands, validation, and rollback guidance.
LogsRelevant raw excerpts and extracted evidence.
MetadataStructured fields captured during log analysis.

9. Jira & Slack Integration

Critical workflow

Log Reader → RCA → Remediation → Cookbook
                         ↓
               Automatic Jira Ticket
                         ↓
               Automatic Slack Summary

Non-critical workflow

Log Reader → RCA → Remediation → Cookbook
                         ↓
              Create Jira Ticket button

Jira ticket contents

  • Incident summary and severity
  • Root cause and evidence
  • Recommendations and confidence
  • Runbook, validation, and rollback
  • Supporting Confluence and Local SOP references
Back to top ↑

10. Supporting Sources and Traceability

Every recommendation should clearly show its grounding evidence.

  • Document title
  • Source type: Confluence or Local SOP
  • Section or heading path
  • Last updated date
  • Source URI or safe file path
  • Similarity or confidence score where available
Traceability should remain visible from log evidence to RCA, recommendation, runbook, Jira ticket, and supporting documentation.
Back to top ↑

11. Best Practices

  • Upload complete log windows, including events before and after the failure.
  • Keep Confluence pages and local SOPs current and approved.
  • Run a manual sync after making urgent SOP or Confluence changes.
  • Review source attribution before accepting a recommendation.
  • Validate generated commands in a safe environment before production use.
  • Use rollback steps whenever remediation changes infrastructure or application state.
  • Confirm that Jira and Slack integrations target the correct project and channel.
Back to top ↑

12. Troubleshooting

No incidents were detected

Verify that the file contains recognizable timestamps, service identifiers, severity indicators, or error events. Upload a larger surrounding log window.

Confluence synchronization failed

Check the site URL, email, scoped API token, Cloud ID, space access, network connectivity, and rate-limit status.

Local SOP documents were not indexed

Confirm the configured directory exists, the backend can read it, recursive scanning is correct, and the file extension is supported.

The sync button is disabled

A manual or scheduled synchronization may already be running. Wait for the active run to complete and refresh the status.

The next scheduled synchronization is missing

The scheduler may be disabled, not configured, unavailable, or not registered during backend startup. Check scheduler status and backend logs.

Recommendations do not contain supporting documents

The vector index may be empty or no sufficiently relevant evidence was found. Run a synchronization and verify retrieval settings.

Jira or Slack integration failed

Verify environment variables, credentials, permissions, project key, channel ID, and outbound network connectivity.

Never place API tokens, passwords, or complete confidential documents in screenshots, tickets, client-side logs, or troubleshooting messages.
Back to top ↑

13. Frequently Asked Questions

Does every incident create a Jira ticket?

No. Critical incidents follow the automatic policy. Non-critical incidents expose a manual Create Jira Ticket action.

Does manual sync rebuild the entire vector database?

No. Normal synchronization is incremental. Unchanged pages and files should not be re-embedded.

What happens when one SOP file fails to parse?

The failure should be reported for that file while the rest of the synchronization continues.

Does a manual sync change the weekly schedule?

No. The weekly schedule remains independent unless an administrator changes the scheduler configuration.

Are recommendations guaranteed to be correct?

No. The application supports incident response by generating evidence-based recommendations, but human review remains required.
